perturbation.original_text: c. take all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information) to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement
perturbation.changed_text: c. take security precautions they deem appropriate to safeguard every part of the Information to prevent it from being disclosed or otherwise made available to any third party except as permitted by this Agreement
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information)' with 'security precautions they deem appropriate' introduces ambiguity. What constitutes 'appropriate' is subjective and can lead to inadequate security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the GDPR mandates that data controllers implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. Deeming security measures as 'appropriate' without reference to specific standards or risk assessments contradicts this requirement, as it allows for subjective interpretation, potentially leading to insufficient data protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Data protection - European Commission Skip to main content Data protection Rules for the protection of personal data inside and outside the EU. Data protection in the EU In today's digital age, where information is constantly shared, collected and processed, there is a need for clear and strong data protection rules. Data protection is a fundamental right under EU law. EU data protection legislation is comprised of the General Data Protection Directive (GDPR), the Law Enforcement Directive (LED), and the Data Protection Regulation for EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies (EUDPR). To ensure that this legislation is applied consistently, national and European data protection authorities and bodies have been established. Find out more about the EU's legal framework for data protection Data protection: questions and answers Data protection explained Read about key concepts such as personal data, data processing, when and to whom the GDPR applies to, and more. Information for individuals Read about the rights you have over your personal data under the GDPR, how to exercise these rights, and more. Information for businesses and organisations Read about data protection principles and obligations, enforcement of the rules, dealing with individuals' requests, and more. Disclaimer The information and guidance in these webpages are intended to contribute to a better understanding of EU data protection rules. This is intended purely as a guidance tool – only the text of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) has legal force. As a consequence, only the GDPR is liable to create rights and obligations for individuals. This guidance does not create any enforceable right or expectation. The binding interpretation of EU legislation is the exclusive competence of the Court of Justice of the European Union. The views expressed in this guidance are without prejudice to the position that the Commission might take before the Court of Justice. Neither the European Commission nor any person acting on behalf of the European Commission is responsible for the use which might be made of the following information. As this guidance reflects the state of the art at the time of its drafting, it should be regarded as a 'living tool' open for improvement and its content may be subject to modifications without notice. International dimension of data protection The EU has established international data protection agreements to ensure that EU citizens' personal data remains protected even if transferred outside the EU. EU data protection legislation includes safeguards for when transferring data to third countries, including adequacy decisions, standard contractual clauses (SCC) and binding corporate rules (BCR). Find out more about the international dimension of data protection Funding to support the implementation of the GDPR The Commission has provided funding to national data protection authorities to finance projects that support the implementation of the GDPR. These projects aim to equip individuals and businesses with the knowledge and resources needed to navigate and ensure compliance with data protection rules. The types of projects funded by the Commission typically include awareness-raising campaigns, training programs, and the development of practical tools and materials that can facilitate small and medium-sized enterprises' (SMEs) compliance with the GDPR. Find out more about these EU-funded projects Simplifying compliance with the GDPR through reduced record-keeping obligations In May 2025, the Commission adopted a Single Market Simplification proposal to cut €300 million in annual administrative cost for companies. In order to respond to calls from Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) and Small and Mid-Caps companies (SMCs), the Commission introduced targeted changes to the rules on record-keeping for small and medium-sized companies and organisations under 750 employees. They include namely: Extending the derogation from record-keep
perturbation.justification: The change from 'all necessary and proper security precautions (and at least as great as those it takes to safeguard its own information)' to 'security precautions they deem appropriate' creates a contradiction. The original text establishes a clear, objective standard for security measures. The changed text introduces a subjective standard, potentially leading to insufficient data protection. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts GDPR Article 32, as it allows for subjective interpretation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports the importance of data protection in the EU, mentioning the GDPR and the need for clear data protection rules. The changed text contradicts the need for clear standards by allowing for a subjective assessment of security precautions. This change weakens the obligation significantly and opens the door to non-compliance, thus a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 4. If a Receiving Party believes it is required by law to disclose any Information under clause 3(d) above, the Receiving Party shall (in each case and to the extent not prohibited in law): a. provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of such requirement or obligation (together with a copy of any relevant access request, court order or other evidence giving rise to such belief) in advance of the required disclosure, to enable the Disclosing Party to seek appropriate protective relief and/or to take other steps to resist or narrow the scope of any required disclosure
perturbation.changed_text: 4. If a Receiving Party believes it is required by law to disclose any Information under clause 3(d) above, the Receiving Party shall (in each case and to the extent not prohibited in law): a. endeavor to provide the Disclosing Party with notice of such requirement or obligation in advance of the required disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice' to 'endeavor to provide the Disclosing Party with notice' weakens the obligation to provide prompt and complete notice. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt, but not a guarantee, of notification, which introduces ambiguity and reduces accountability. Removing the obligation to provide 'a copy of any relevant access request, court order or other evidence giving rise to such belief' means the Disclosing Party could not assess the validity of the disclosure requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is US law, this is the UK equivalent. This rule mandates specific procedures for disclosure, including providing adequate notice and documentation. By stating the Receiving Party only needs to 'endeavor' to provide notice and removing the need to provide documentation, the modification contradicts the CPR's requirements for transparency and due process in legal disclosures. The alteration makes compliance discretionary, conflicting with the mandated process of providing full documentation and prompt notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal conviction or sentence; (iv) an action brought without an attorney by a person in the custody of the United States, a state, or a state subdivision; (v) an action to enforce or quash an administrative summons or subpoena; (vi) an action by the United States to recover benefit payments; (vii) an action by the United States to collect on a student loan guaranteed by the United States; (viii) a proceeding ancillary to a proceeding in another court; and (ix) an action to enforce an arbitration award. (C) Time for Initial Disclosures—In General. A party must make the initial disclosures at or within 14 days after the parties’ Rule 26(f) conference unless a different time is set by stipulation or court order, or unless a party objects during the conference that initial disclosures are not appropriate in this action and states the objection in the proposed discovery plan. In ruling on the objection, the court must determine what disclosures, if any, are to be made and must set the time for disclosure. (D) Time for Initial Disclosures—For Parties Served or Joined Later. A party that is first served or otherwise joined after the Rule 26(f) conference must make the initial disclosures within 30 days after being served or joined, unless a different time is set by stipulation or court order. (E) Basis for Initial Disclosure; Unacceptable Excuses. A party must make its initial disclosures based on the information then reasonably available to it. A party is not excused from making its disclosures because it has not fully investigated the case or because it challenges the sufficiency of another party's disclosures or because another party has not made its disclosures. (2) Disclosure of Expert Testimony. (A) In General. In addition to the disclosures required by Rule 26(a)(1) , a party must disclose to the other parties the identity of any witness it may use at trial to present evidence under Federal Rule of Evidence 702 , 703 , or 705 . (B) Witnesses Who Must Provide a Written Repor
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'provide the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice' to 'endeavor to provide the Disclosing Party with notice' weakens the obligation, creating a legal contradiction. Rule 26(a)(1)(A) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, as provided in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates specific disclosures including 'the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information...'. By changing the language to 'endeavor', the Receiving Party is no longer strictly obligated to provide prompt written notice or any documentation, potentially hindering the Disclosing Party's ability to seek protective relief as initially intended by the original contract, undermining the standard of transparency and due process inherent in legal disclosures.

perturbation.original_text: 5. The ETI shall be entitled to disclose or make available any Information it receives from the Respondents (or any of them) to: a. such of the ETI Affiliates, and either the ETI’s or the ETI Affiliates’ employees, officers, secondees, agent s, consultants, sub -contractors, proposed sub -contractors, professional advisers and proposed professional advisers where such disclosure is necessary for the Purpose, provided that all such aforementioned persons to whom any Information is disclosed by th e ETI are bound by obligations of confidentiality and the ETI shall be responsible for breaches of the obligations by such persons and each ETI Affiliate may enforce this clause in accordance with the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999
perturbation.changed_text: 5. The ETI may disclose or make available any Information it receives from the Respondents (or any of them) to: a. such of the ETI Affiliates, and either the ETI’s or the ETI Affiliates’ employees, officers, secondees, agent s, consultants, sub -contractors, proposed sub -contractors, professional advisers and proposed professional advisers where such disclosure is reasonably believed to be necessary for the Purpose, provided that the ETI will encourage such persons to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may disclose' makes the disclosure discretionary rather than a right. Replacing 'where such disclosure is necessary for the Purpose' with 'where such disclosure is reasonably believed to be necessary for the Purpose' introduces a subjective element – 'reasonably believed' – allowing the ETI to disclose information even if it is not strictly necessary. Changing the phrase: 'provided that all such aforementioned persons to whom any Information is disclosed by the ETI are bound by obligations of confidentiality and the ETI shall be responsible for breaches of the obligations by such persons' to 'provided that the ETI will encourage such persons to maintain confidentiality' removes any assurance that the ETI will ensure confidentiality amongst its affiliates and employees, or be responsible for breaches by them.
perturbation.law_citation: Regulation 3 of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 mandates that trade secret holders take reasonable steps to keep information secret. By changing 'shall be responsible for breaches of the obligations by such persons' to 'provided that the ETI will encourage such persons to maintain confidentiality,' this removes the guarantee that ETI will take reasonable steps to maintain confidentiality of trade secrets by making sure the affiliates are held accountable and responsible.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. The original text states that individuals are 'bound by obligations of confidentiality and the ETI shall be responsible for breaches of the obligations'. The changed text replaces this with 'the ETI will encourage such persons to maintain confidentiality,' removing the guarantee of confidentiality and accountability. The Law Explanation states Regulation 3 of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 mandates that trade secret holders take reasonable steps to keep information secret. By removing the responsibility for breaches, the changed text could be interpreted as not taking reasonable steps to maintain confidentiality as the ETI is no longer responsible for breaches, directly contradicting the intention of the law as explained. Snippet 1 shows the name of the law, Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018, which corroborates the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 13. The Parties agree that the ETI may disclose that one or more of the Respondents are involved in discussions with the ETI and the subject matter of the discussions provided that the ETI will provide a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents and seek the approva l of the Respondents prior to its publication or release. Other than as set out in this clause, none of the Parties will make any public announcements, statements or otherwise publicise the subject matter of this Agreement (or its existence) without the pr ior written consent of the other Parties and no Party will use the business names or trade marks of any other Party in any way without that Party’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. The Parties agree that the ETI may disclose that one or more of the Respondents are involved in discussions with the ETI and the subject matter of the discussions provided that the ETI will attempt to provide a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents prior to its publication or release. Other than as set out in this clause, none of the Parties will make any public announcements, statements or otherwise publicise the subject matter of this Agreement (or its existence) without what they consider reasonable consultation with the other Parties and no Party will use the business names or trade marks of any other Party in any way without consulting that Party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the ETI will provide a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents and seek the approval of the Respondents prior to its publication or release' with 'the ETI will attempt to provide a copy of any press release or other announcement to the Respondents prior to its publication or release' weakens the obligation. Attempting to provide a copy is not the same as actually providing one and seeking approval. Changing the phrase 'prior written consent' to 'what they consider reasonable consultation' for public announcements, statements or otherwise publicising the agreement, is vague, introduces subjectivity, and weakens the obligation to obtain consent. Similarly, changing 'without that Party's prior written consent' to 'without consulting that Party' when using business names or trademarks creates uncertainty and reduces legal protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Defamation Act 2013 deals primarily with defamation claims, it also indirectly relates to the unauthorized disclosure of confidential information that could harm a party's reputation. By changing the requirement for prior written consent to 'what they consider reasonable consultation,' the modified text increases the risk of unauthorized disclosures that could potentially be defamatory. The original clause provides a clear mechanism to prevent potentially defamatory statements; the modification weakens this protection, increasing the potential for breaches of confidentiality that could lead to a defamation claim. Section 1 requires that a statement has caused or is likely to cause serious harm to the reputation of the claimant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Defamation Act 2013 – 6 months on - 5RB Barristers Menu Barristers Cases By Jonathan Barnes KC 1 Jul 2014 James Price QC , Jonathan Barnes and Yuli Takatsuki will be reviewing the first few months in force of the Defamation Act 2013 at Conference 5RB on 30 September 2014. Here they outline some of the themes for discussion. Qn: The Defamation Act 2013 came into force on 1 st January 2014, billed as providing enhanced protection for publishers and website operators, and with measures to curb trivial claims and “libel tourism”. Has it been a success? Jonathan: It is too early to say in terms of judicial decisions, with no cases so far decided under the Act. This probably reflects simply that the Act has no retrospective effect. Defamation complaints arising since the beginning of this year are still being formulated and will be in their relatively early stages. However, anecdotal accounts suggest that potential company claimants are treating the statutory requirement of serious harm introduced by section 1 as a significant bar to seeking relief, where they are not able to identify readily any actual financial loss arising as a result of defamatory allegations made about them. This is not actually what section 1(2) of the Act says, contemplating as it does that a company may suffer serious harm where it is likely to be caused serious financial loss by something said about it. But it may be that, in the early days of the Act, some corporate claimant advisers have swallowed too much of the defendant hype that featured particularly during the lobbying stages of the Bill. James: There is a preliminary issue pending in an action brought by a non-profit corporation and its chief executive, in which the claimants have little actual evidence of particular harm. So the question for the court is whether it is enough to satisfy the “serious harm to reputation” test in s.1 of the Act that the allegation can be regarded as a very serious one, widely circulated in a national newspaper. The issue may n
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the ETI to provide a copy of any press release and seek approval from the respondents before publication. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating that the ETI will only 'attempt' to provide a copy. Furthermore, changing 'prior written consent' to 'what they consider reasonable consultation' and 'without that Party’s prior written consent' to 'without consulting that Party' weakens the obligation to obtain consent, making it more ambiguous. The Law Explanation links this change to the Defamation Act 2013, arguing that the weakened obligation increases the risk of unauthorized disclosures that could be defamatory. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions the Defamation Act 2013 and the 'serious harm to reputation' test, suggesting the potential for legal ramifications if confidential information is disclosed without proper consent, as the changes introduce ambiguity and reduce the control parties have over announcements. The changes create an ambiguous legal obligation because the original terms have been weakened and made vague.
